learning_outcomes.description: Apply core concepts of the new product development process to solve problems and address real-world scenarios in food product development.
Use knowledge of the new product development process to design a product development Brief.
Review food product ingredients to determine nutrition information.
Demonstrate core practical skills in new food product development using laboratory techniques which require both qualitative and quantitative outcomes.
Communicate findings appropriate for the food product development field in written format.
Apply effective teamwork and communication skills to achieve a common outcome.

assessments.assessment_title: Sequential New Food Product Design Brief Report
assessments.weighting: <dd>15% This Sequential task is comprised of Idea Generation (1.5%), Concept Statement (1.5%) and New Product Design Brief Report (Weighted 12%) for a total of 15% </dd>
assessments.due_date:
Milestone 1: Idea generation (Weighted 1.5%) 14/08/2026 2:00 pm

Milestone 2: Concept statement (Weighted 1.5%) 28/08/2026 2:00 pm

New Food Product Design Brief Report (Weighted 12%) 11/09/2026 2:00 pm
assessments.task_description:
Task Description:

This is a group assignment. Each group comprising five (5) students (self-selected using the course Blackboard Ultra site) are required to develop a detailed product design brief for a proposed new food product, by applying concepts and knowledge developed throughout lectures and tutorials. The report should include analysis and interpretation of market research results, idea generation and justification of the selected concept, concept statement, idea screening and evaluation process, and a comprehensive product brief that will serve as the blueprint for the proposed new product. Students are expected to apply food science, consumer, and market knowledge in developing the product concept, with decisions and justifications supported by relevant literature, market trends, and/or industry evidence where appropriate. This assessment represents the planning and concept development stage of the semester-long New Product Development project and will provide the foundation for subsequent prototype development and evaluation activities later in the course. Assessment will consider both the quality of the final report and the successful completion of project milestones throughout the semester, including the idea generation stage and the product's concept statement. The report should be 1200–1500 words in length (excluding references and appendices).

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use Artificial Intelligence (AI) and/or Machine Translation (MT) tools, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI or MT will provide only limited support and guidance. 

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. 

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. 


Report Contents:

The report will contain the following section:

  • Title page - including group members' names and ID and product name.
  • Declaration of AI or MT use (if any).
  • Introduction
  • Idea generation
  • Screening process and selected idea
  • Concept statement
  • Detailed product brief of the selected idea: should provide elaborate explanation for each feature of the selected idea.
  • Reference list. It is suggested to use APA 7th referencing style.
  • Appendix (if any).

Further details on the report structure and guidelines are provided via Blackboard Ultra.


Assessment Breakdown:

  • Milestone achievements: 3% (each milestone weigthed 1.5%)
  • New Food Product Design Brief Report: 12%

Marking rubric and criteria are provided via Blackboard Ultra


Tutorial Attendance:

As tutorial activities support the development of the product concept and brief, regular attendance and active participation in tutorials are strongly recommended to support successful completion of the assessment tasks. Refer to specific Tutorial and Practical Attendance requirements in Additional Course Grading Information.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You must submit your Assessment task, in Blackboard Ultra, via the Turnitin link by the submission deadline. You should also retain an electronic copy of every piece of assessment you submit.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
Task Description:

This is an individual assignment. Students will be provided with a randomly assigned food product formulation and will be required to develop components of a food label in accordance with relevant food regulatory requirements and standards. Following the label development, students are required to critically evaluate the product formulation and completed label to identify and justify any claims that may be made under FSANZ regulations, including nutritional claims, health claims, and other permitted claims where applicable. Students must demonstrate understanding of ingredient composition, nutrient calculations, and regulatory requirements when determining the suitability of these claims. The assignment will involve interpretation and application of food labelling regulations, including preparation of mandatory labelling information and evaluation of the suitability of claims that may be associated with the product. The assessment is designed to develop students’ understanding of food labelling compliance, nutrient composition, and regulatory considerations in food product development. Marking rubric and criteria are provided via Blackboard Ultra.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use Artificial Intelligence (AI) and/or Machine Translation (MT) tools, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI or MT will provide only limited support and guidance. 

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. 

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. 

The use of AI or MT must be referenced appropriately. Please see assessment guidelines in blackboard course site for specific instructions.

Report Contents and Details:

  • Title page - including student name and ID and formulation number.
  • Declaration of AI or MT use (if any).
  • Parts of mandatory food label - including nutrition information panel (NIP), ingredient list, allergen summary statement and claims.
  • Explanation and justifications on the claims included in the label.
  • Explanations and calculations used to create the NIP.
  • Reference list. It is suggested to use APA 7th referencing style.
  • Appendix - including, but not limited to, label of the custom ingredients, reference product, and others.

Further details of the report are provided via Blackboard Ultra.

assessments.task_description:
Task Description:

This assessment requires students to work in groups (the same group from New Food Product Design Brief Report ). During practical classes, students will work through the stages of the New Product Development (NPD) process to develop a product prototype within an assigned category, guided by the product brief developed earlier. Groups will refine their formulation, processing methods, and product characteristics through practical development and evaluation activities. The final report should document and justify the development process undertaken by the group, including formulation decisions, processing development, sensory evaluation, packaging considerations, and scale-up considerations. Students are expected to apply food science, consumer, and market knowledge throughout the project, with decisions and justifications supported by relevant literature and experimental observations. Each member of the group will be responsible and graded for writing one part of the report as well as contributing to the overall report. Assessment will consider both the quality of the final report and the successful completion of prototype development milestones. Additionally, a peer assessment will be used to evaluate contributions.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use Artificial Intelligence (AI) and/or Machine Translation (MT) tools, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI or MT will provide only limited support and guidance. 

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. 

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. 

The use of AI or MT must be referenced appropriately. Please see assessment guidelines in blackboard course site for specific instructions.


Report Structure:

The report will contain the following section:

  • Title page
  • Legal declaration
  • Introduction - written by the group (word count limit: 300 ± 10% words)
  • Ingredient selection and formulation - written by Student 1
  • Process development and cost calculation - written by Student 2
  • Sensory attributes and evaluation - written by Student 3
  • Packaging, labelling and shelf life - written by Student 4
  • Scale up - written by Student 5
  • Reflection - written by the group (word count limit: 250 ± 10% words)

All parts of the report must be compiled and submitted as one report.

Word limit: each individually written part should have 1000 ± 10% words, excluding references and appendix.

Each of the individually written part must have its own reference list, using APA 7th referencing style. Further details on the report structure, templates and guidelines are provided via Blackboard Ultra.


Assessment Breakdown:

Final report: 25%, consist of:

  • Individual part of the report: 20%
  • Group part of the report: 3%
  • Peer assessment: 2%

Marking rubric and criteria are provided via Blackboard Ultra


Practical Attendance

As product prototype development and evaluation are conducted during practical classes, attendance and active participation in practical sessions are essential for successful completion of the final report. Refer to specific Tutorial and Practical Attendance requirements in Additional Course Grading Information.

assessments.task_description:
The End of Semester Examination will assess the level of theoretical knowledge and understanding of the basic principles of new product development processes. The exam will cover all the lecture materials.

The exam will be an on-campus and in person. Have your UQ student ID card available for all your exams.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(27 Jul - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Lectures

Lectures are delivered to cover the various stages involved in product development as well as the food regulations and approval procedure for new products. A systematic product development approach is discussed to maximise the chances of success of new food products in the marketplace. A detailed week by week timetable of lecture topics can be accessed under learning activities section in the course Blackboard Ultra site.


Sub-activity: Formal techniques and process in development of new food product. Best practice in new product development. Roles of customer in new product development. Food regulations and FSANZ approval for new products, food labelling.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Lectures Lectures are delivered to cover the various stages involved in product development as well as the food regulations and approval procedure for new products. A systematic product development approach is discussed to maximise the chances of success of new food products in the marketplace. A detailed week by week timetable of lecture topics can be accessed under learning activities section in the course Blackboard Ultra site. Sub-activity: Formal techniques and process in development of new food product. Best practice in new product development. Roles of customer in new product development. Food regulations and FSANZ approval for new products, food labelling.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 2 To Week 6(03 Aug - 06 Sep)
learning_activities.topic:
Tutorials

The students will have the opportunity to put the theory learnt in lectures into practice during the tutorials. As groups, the students will design a new food product. Reporting will be via a written report. A detailed week by week timetable of tutorial topics can be accessed in the course Blackboard Ultra site.


Sub-activity: Planning for new product development, starting from idea generation until ingredient selections.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Tutorials The students will have the opportunity to put the theory learnt in lectures into practice during the tutorials. As groups, the students will design a new food product. Reporting will be via a written report. A detailed week by week timetable of tutorial topics can be accessed in the course Blackboard Ultra site. Sub-activity: Planning for new product development, starting from idea generation until ingredient selections.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 7 To Week 13(07 Sep - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Practicals

From Week 7 onwards, the students will have the opportunity to come in the labs to create a prototype of their product.


Sub-activity: Developing prototype for new food product based on the plan developed during tutorials.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
